# Automated prompt design framework (2026-02-11 22:13Z)

_**User**_

Go through the folder files. This is mys current Framework to design and improve system prompts.
I run this in CHAT GPT.

What I want:
I want a project that alllows me to run this proccess async (not in GPT) 

Why:
I'm currently the only team member to design the prompts. I have to many agents to design.
I need an atumated agentic system to off load tasks

High-level spces:
- process and distill all information into a prompt design framework.
- set 3 different design patterns based on the dosumentation in this folders.
- systematic system structure common to every prompt (rule blocks, flows, execution plan, etc...)
- agenst and sub agents that work autonomously
- possibility to run agent as evaluators ( Anthropic and Google Gemini)
- should be built on Open Ai API and agents SDK if it makes sense.
- Python would be my go to dev enviroment.

```json
{
  "title": "Clarifying scope for your prompt-automation project",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "scope_mvp",
      "prompt": "For the first version, what is the main thing you want this system to do end-to-end without you in the loop: (a) take a new agent idea and produce a draft system prompt following your framework, (b) refactor/upgrade existing prompts into your orthogonal format, or (c) run batch evaluations of prompts across OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini?",
      "options": [
        {
          "id": "a_new_prompt",
          "label": "Generate new system prompts from an agent idea/spec"
        },
        {
          "id": "b_refactor_existing",
          "label": "Refactor/upgrade existing prompts into the framework"
        },
        {
          "id": "c_evaluate_prompts",
          "label": "Run batch evaluations of prompts across providers"
        },
        {
          "id": "d_combination",
          "label": "A combination of these (I’ll specify)"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "io_surface",
      "prompt": "Where do you ideally want to author specs and see outputs for this system in the near term?",
      "options": [
        {
          "id": "cli_local",
          "label": "Pure local CLI / scripts in Python"
        },
        {
          "id": "notion_md",
          "label": "Markdown files / Notion-style docs as inputs &amp; outputs"
        },
        {
          "id": "web_ui",
          "label": "A small web UI (e.g. FastAPI + simple frontend)"
        },
        {
          "id": "no_strong_pref",
          "label": "No strong preference, optimize for speed to MVP"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

You want an MVP that, from a Markdown/Notion-style spec of a new agent, automatically generates a first-pass orthogonal system prompt; I’ll shape the architecture and plan around that, with evaluation and refactoring as later extensions. Now I’ll propose a concrete Python + OpenAI/Agents-based project structure and agent topology that encodes your Task/Plan/Rule Blocks model and supports Anthropic/Gemini evaluators.

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="create_plan">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **create_plan**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "plan": "## Goal\n\nBuild a Python project (library + CLI, optionally embeddable in Notion/Markdown workflows) that:\n- Ingests an agent spec written in natural language/Markdown.\n- Distills it through your orthogonal framework (Task, Execution Plan, Rule Blocks) and tiered model.\n- Uses OpenAI (and later Anthropic/Gemini) to generate and iterate on system prompts.\n- Supports autonomous agents/sub‑agents for design, refinement, and evaluation.\n\n## High-level architecture\n\n- **Core library (&#96;prompt_framework/&#96;)**\n  - **&#96;models/&#96;**: Pydantic (or dataclasses) for domain models: &#96;AgentSpec&#96;, &#96;PromptTier&#96;, &#96;TaskBlock&#96;, &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96;, &#96;RuleBlockRef&#96;, &#96;FullPromptDesign&#96;.\n  - **&#96;parsers/&#96;**: Parsers that read Markdown/Notion-style inputs and map them into &#96;AgentSpec&#96; objects using your graph docs as reference (e.g. sections for role, constraints, examples).\n  - **&#96;patterns/&#96;**: Encoded design patterns derived from your framework in &#96;[CLAUDE.md](/Volumes/Dev_Projects/Superloop/_DOCS_RESEARCH_LEARNINGS/CLAUDE.md)&#96;, &#96;[Graph/01-System-Prompt-Design-Framework.md](/Volumes/Dev_Projects/Superloop/_DOCS_RESEARCH_LEARNINGS/Graph/01-System-Prompt-Design-Framework.md)&#96;, and tiered model; three Python-level templates:\n    - Tier 1: Task-only template.\n    - Tier 2: Task + minimal Execution Plan.\n    - Tier 3: Full Task + Execution Plan + named Rule Blocks.\n  - **&#96;renderers/&#96;**: Functions to render &#96;FullPromptDesign&#96; into concrete system-prompt text (for ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini), respecting orthogonality (Task section, Plan section, Rule Blocks referenced by stable names, no duplication).\n  - **&#96;agents/&#96;**: Orchestrated agents:\n    - &#96;DesignAgent&#96;: maps an &#96;AgentSpec&#96; + tier choice to a first-pass &#96;FullPromptDesign&#96; using OpenAI Agents SDK.\n    - &#96;RefinementAgent&#96;: takes feedback or evaluation signals and proposes diffs to Task/Plan/Rule Blocks, not raw text blobs.\n    - &#96;EvaluatorAgent&#96;: wraps calls to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini to run test conversations and compute quality metrics using your rule blocks.\n  - **&#96;providers/&#96;**: Thin abstraction for model APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini) with pluggable backends and unified interface.\n  - **&#96;storage/&#96;**: File-based persistence of specs and prompt designs as Markdown/JSON (later extensible to Notion or a DB).\n\n- **CLI (&#96;promptctl&#96;)**\n  - &#96;promptctl init-spec&#96;: scaffold a new agent spec Markdown based on templates from your graph (e.g. role, context, inputs, outputs).\n  - &#96;promptctl design --spec path.md --tier [1|2|3]&#96;: run the DesignAgent and save a rendered system prompt + structured JSON.\n  - &#96;promptctl evaluate --design design.json --scenario scenarios.md --providers openai,anthropic,gemini&#96;: run EvaluatorAgents and output a report.\n\n- **Optional integrations (later phases)**\n  - Notion sync (read/write pages that contain specs and designs).\n  - Lightweight FastAPI service that exposes the same operations via HTTP, enabling UI or other tools.\n\n## Mapping your framework into code\n\n- **Orthogonal components as code entities**\n  - &#96;TaskBlock&#96;: encapsulates WHAT (outcomes, constraints, acceptance criteria) with no execution rules.\n  - &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96;: ordered steps, branching, validation logic; no formatting/schema rules.\n  - &#96;RuleBlock&#96;: separate, named objects that store schemas, citation rules, markup; defined in Python + persisted as Markdown using content from your rule-block docs like &#96;[Graph/05-Rule-Blocks-Component.md](/Volumes/Dev_Projects/Superloop/_DOCS_RESEARCH_LEARNINGS/Graph/05-Rule-Blocks-Component.md).\n  - &#96;PromptDesign&#96;: ties them together: &#96;tier&#96;, &#96;task&#96;, &#96;plan&#96;, &#96;rule_block_refs: list[str]&#96;.\n\n- **Tiered complexity model in code**\n  - &#96;PromptTier&#96; enum: &#96;TIER_1&#96;, &#96;TIER_2&#96;, &#96;TIER_3&#96; (mirroring your tier docs linked from &#96;[Graph/00-Index.md](/Volumes/Dev_Projects/Superloop/_DOCS_RESEARCH_LEARNINGS/Graph/00-Index.md)).\n  - &#96;PatternSelector&#96;: given &#96;AgentSpec&#96; (scope, determinism requirements, output schema) chooses tier and design pattern.\n  - Each tier backed by a dedicated pattern class with clear responsibilities:\n    - &#96;Tier1Pattern.build(agent_spec) -&gt; PromptDesign&#96; (just Task).\n    - &#96;Tier2Pattern.build(agent_spec) -&gt; PromptDesign&#96; (Task + minimal Plan).\n    - &#96;Tier3Pattern.build(agent_spec) -&gt; PromptDesign&#96; (full Task, Plan, Rule Blocks referencing citation/guardrail schemas derived from your brief-agent docs like &#96;breif-generator-prompt-26-02-2026.md&#96;).\n\n- **Rule Blocks as a registry**\n  - A &#96;rule_blocks/&#96; directory containing small, focused Markdown files per block (e.g. &#96;citation_rules.md&#96;, &#96;html_markup_rules.md&#96;, &#96;json_schema_brief.md&#96;).\n  - A &#96;RuleBlockRegistry&#96; class that loads these files, indexes them by name, and exposes them to agents as reference-only content.\n  - DesignAgent and EvaluatorAgent only ever refer to them by name and content snippets, never duplicating their definitions.\n\n## Agent and sub-agent workflow\n\n- **1. Spec ingestion**\n  - User writes a spec in Markdown/Notion style (role, goals, inputs, outputs, constraints, examples).\n  - &#96;AgentSpecParser&#96; reads the file, extracting fields, applying light heuristics or an OpenAI model to classify agent type (sales, brief-writing, coding, etc.) using knowledge from &#96;[Graph/12-Agent-Types.md](/Volumes/Dev_Projects/Superloop/_DOCS_RESEARCH_LEARNINGS/Graph/12-Agent-Types.md).\n\n- **2. DesignAgent (OpenAI-based)**\n  - Takes &#96;AgentSpec + PromptTier + RuleBlockRegistry&#96; as input.\n  - Uses OpenAI Agents SDK to:\n    - Propose a structured &#96;TaskBlock&#96; aligned with your placement router rules from the framework.\n    - Propose an &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96; when tier ≥ 2, ensuring no rule leakage.\n    - Select appropriate &#96;RuleBlock&#96; names based on agent type (e.g. citation vs no-citation agents).\n  - Output is a structured &#96;PromptDesign&#96; JSON plus a rendered system-prompt string.\n\n- **3. RefinementAgent**\n  - Accepts &#96;PromptDesign&#96; plus either human feedback or evaluation metrics.\n  - Uses model calls to suggest incremental modifications (e.g. \"move this constraint from Plan to Rule Block\", \"tighten acceptance criteria\"), returning diffs at the object level.\n\n- **4. EvaluatorAgents (multi-provider)**\n  - For each provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini):\n    - Wrap prompt + sample conversations into evaluation jobs.\n    - Apply rule blocks as scoring criteria (e.g. compliance with citation rules, guardrails, structure).\n  - Returns:\n    - Per-provider metrics (accuracy proxies, structure compliance, guardrail violations).\n    - Example failures, wired back into RefinementAgent suggestions.\n\n## Technology choices\n\n- **Language &amp; tooling**\n  - Python 3.11+.\n  - Dependency management with &#96;uv&#96; or &#96;poetry&#96;.\n  - Pydantic for models, Typer for CLI, optional FastAPI for HTTP layer.\n\n- **LLM providers**\n  - OpenAI as the primary design engine (Agents SDK for orchestration and tool calls).\n  - Anthropic &amp; Gemini adapters that speak their respective SDKs but implement a shared &#96;LLMClient&#96; interface.\n\n- **Persistence &amp; I/O surface**\n  - Store specs and designs as Markdown + JSON under a &#96;projects/&#96; directory to keep everything inspectable and Git-friendly.\n  - Option to import/export Notion pages later via Notion API; keep internal representation neutral (Markdown-first).\n\n## Implementation phases &amp; tasks\n\n- **Phase 1: Skeleton &amp; domain modeling**\n  - Set up Python project structure (&#96;prompt_framework&#96; package, basic &#96;pyproject.toml&#96; or &#96;requirements.txt&#96;).\n  - Implement domain models: &#96;AgentSpec&#96;, &#96;PromptTier&#96;, &#96;TaskBlock&#96;, &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96;, &#96;RuleBlock&#96;, &#96;PromptDesign&#96;.\n  - Create initial CLI with no LLM calls yet: &#96;promptctl init-spec&#96; and &#96;promptctl design --dry-run&#96; that just echoes parsed spec + chosen tier.\n\n- **Phase 2: Encode your framework &amp; patterns**\n  - Read and distill key docs (&#96;CLAUDE.md&#96;, &#96;Graph/01-System-Prompt-Design-Framework.md&#96;, &#96;Graph/05-Rule-Blocks-Component.md&#96;, brief-agent prompt) into concrete Python-level pattern classes for Tier 1/2/3.\n  - Implement &#96;RuleBlockRegistry&#96; with a few core rule blocks (citation, HTML formatting, JSON schema examples) as Markdown resources.\n  - Implement &#96;PatternSelector&#96; that chooses tier and pattern based on spec metadata.\n\n- **Phase 3: OpenAI DesignAgent**\n  - Integrate OpenAI API + Agents SDK.\n  - Implement &#96;DesignAgent&#96; that:\n    - Calls an OpenAI agent with your framework docs and &#96;AgentSpec&#96; to generate &#96;TaskBlock&#96; / &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96; / &#96;RuleBlock&#96; selection.\n    - Validates outputs against basic schema rules (types, presence of key fields).\n    - Writes both structured JSON and rendered system prompt to disk.\n\n- **Phase 4: Evaluator and refinement agents**\n  - Implement &#96;EvaluatorAgent&#96; for OpenAI first: run sample test conversations (defined in a &#96;scenarios.md&#96; file) and score outputs against rule blocks.\n  - Hook in Anthropic and Gemini via provider adapters (configurable via env vars).\n  - Implement &#96;RefinementAgent&#96; that uses evaluation reports to suggest improved &#96;PromptDesign&#96; objects.\n\n- **Phase 5: Notion/Markdown workflow polish**\n  - Add helpers to:\n    - Generate Markdown summaries of designs (for docs and sharing).\n    - Optionally sync designs/specs to Notion pages.\n  - Add a minimal FastAPI service if you want a web endpoint later (e.g. &#96;POST /design&#96;, &#96;POST /evaluate&#96;).\n\n## Todos\n\n- **proj-structure**: Scaffold the Python project (&#96;prompt_framework&#96; package, CLI entrypoint &#96;promptctl&#96;, basic config for OpenAI keys).\n- **domain-models**: Implement core domain models (&#96;AgentSpec&#96;, &#96;PromptTier&#96;, &#96;TaskBlock&#96;, &#96;ExecutionPlanBlock&#96;, &#96;RuleBlock&#96;, &#96;PromptDesign&#96;).\n- **parsers-spec**: Implement Markdown spec parser to extract role, goals, inputs, outputs, constraints, and examples into &#96;AgentSpec&#96;.\n- **patterns-tiers**: Encode Tier 1/2/3 design patterns based on your framework docs and connect to &#96;PatternSelector&#96;.\n- **rule-registry**: Create &#96;RuleBlockRegistry&#96; backed by small Markdown rule-block files reflecting your existing rule-block documentation.\n- **design-agent**: Implement OpenAI-based &#96;DesignAgent&#96; that produces a first-pass &#96;PromptDesign&#96; and rendered system prompt.\n- **evaluator-agents**: Implement &#96;EvaluatorAgent&#96; abstraction and provider-specific evaluators for OpenAI, then Anthropic and Gemini.\n- **refinement-agent**: Implement &#96;RefinementAgent&#96; that consumes evaluation feedback and produces structured diffs to &#96;PromptDesign&#96;.\n- **cli-commands**: Flesh out &#96;promptctl&#96; commands (&#96;init-spec&#96;, &#96;design&#96;, &#96;evaluate&#96;) wired into the agents and storage.\n- **notion-integration**: (Optional) Add Notion integration for reading specs and writing designs as pages once core flow is stable.",
```

### How I’ll incorporate this into the plan

- **Python standards**: I’ll treat `python_best_practices_guide.md` as the authoritative coding standard for the project (function ≤ 20 lines, no magic values, externalized templates, strong typing, dependency injection, etc.) and design modules/abstractions to match its \"AI iteration trinity\" and role-separation ideas.
- **Tooling**: The project plan will explicitly use **uv** for dependency management and **ruff** for linting/formatting (and I’ll assume ruff also enforces many of your style rules where possible).
- **Architecture alignment**: The earlier plan (prompt-framework core + agents + CLI) already matches your \"AI Agentic System Architecture\" section; I’ll enforce that prompts, eval datasets, and context pipelines live in external files/directories (e.g. `ai_components/prompts/`, `ai_components/evals/`) so prompt designers and context engineers can work without code changes.

If you’d like, next step I can:  
- Refine the plan file to call out `uv` + `ruff` explicitly in the project-structure task, and  
- Sketch a concrete directory layout (packages, `ai_components/` folders, and where rule-block and template files live) consistent with this guide.

### What’s in place now

- **Project scaffolded** at the docs root with `pyproject.toml`, `README.md`, and a Python package `prompt_design_system` that follows your Python best-practices guide and is ready for `uv` + `ruff`.
- **Core models** implemented in `models.py`: `PromptTier`, `TaskBlock`, `ExecutionStep`, `ExecutionPlanBlock`, `RuleBlockRef`, `AgentSpec`, and `PromptDesign`, directly mirroring your Task / Plan / Rule Blocks orthogonality.
- **Infrastructure** added: `config.py` for centralized paths (`ai_components/…` for prompts, rule blocks, evals) and a filesystem-backed `RuleBlockRegistry` in `rule_blocks.py` for small Markdown rule-block files.
- **Minimal CLI** wired via `promptctl` in `pyproject.toml`: `init-spec` generates a Markdown agent-spec skeleton; `design` currently creates a placeholder `PromptDesign` (Tier 1–3 flag) and renders a very simple system-prompt header, ready to be upgraded into the full DesignAgent flow next.

### 1. Is this production‑ready and is the structure “good”?

**Short answer:** it’s a **solid, clean starting point**, but **not yet production‑ready**. The folder structure is *acceptable* for an MVP, but you’d typically tighten it a bit for a long‑lived production project.

What’s good already:

- **Clear core package**: `prompt_design_system/` with `models.py`, `config.py`, `rule_blocks.py`, `cli.py` is coherent and small.
- **Centralized config**: paths and `ai_components/` are cleanly abstracted in `AppConfig` / `AppPaths`.
- **Externalized behavior**: `ai_components/` is reserved for prompts/rule blocks/evals, matching your AI‑architecture and Python best‑practices guide.
- **Modern tooling**: `pyproject.toml` with `uv`/`ruff`/`pytest` hooks is aligned with your standards.
- **Function style**: new code is short, typed, and avoids magic values.

What’s missing for “production‑ready”:

- **Tests + layout**: no `tests/` package yet; for production you want at least smoke tests for CLI and core models.
- **Structured logging & error handling**: CLI exits are minimal; a production tool should have consistent logging, well‑typed exceptions, and user‑friendly error messages.
- **Config/environment handling**: no OpenAI/other provider config yet (no `AppConfig.from_env()`/`LLMConfig` etc.).
- **Provider abstraction**: no `providers/` module for OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini yet, so agent orchestration is still just a plan.
- **Repository layout**: you’re mixing docs and code at the same level. For a mature structure, I’d recommend something like:
  - `prompt_design_system/` (or better, `src/prompt_design_system/`) – code
  - `ai_components/` – prompts, rule blocks, evals
  - `_DOCS/` – all your design docs
  - `tests/` – tests
  - top‑level `pyproject.toml`, `README.md`

Within your current repo, that’s mostly cosmetic, but it’s worth doing before you grow the codebase.

### 2. Abstracting rule blocks and design patterns from `_DOCS`

Your expectation is exactly right, and it fits the architecture we started:

- **Rule blocks (Markdown files under `ai_components/rule_blocks/`)**  
  From `_DOCS` and `Graph/*`, we would extract **reusable, named rule blocks**, for example:
  - `citation_rules.md` – from the citation system sections in the brief generator prompt.
  - `html_markup_rules.md` – markup/highlighting rules (allowed tags, section structure).
  - `brief_structure_rules.md` – required sections, ordering, required placeholders like `TBD`/`MISSING`.
  - `guardrail_brand_vimeo.md` – the Vimeo‑specific brand guardrail block.
  - `json_schema_brief.md` – any structured brief schemas you’ve defined.
  These become the canonical **Rule Blocks** that `RuleBlockRegistry` loads and your prompts reference by name only.

- **Design patterns (Python under `prompt_design_system/patterns/`)**  
  From `_DOCS/Prompt_Design_Framework`, `prompt-TKDSL-workframe.prompt-design.md`, and your existing system‑prompt examples, we’d implement:
  - `Tier1Pattern`, `Tier2Pattern`, `Tier3Pattern` classes that:
    - Take an `AgentSpec` and set of `RuleBlock` names.
    - Produce a `PromptDesign` that respects your placement router (what belongs in Task vs Plan vs Rule Blocks).
  - Optional **agent‑type patterns** (using `Graph/12-Agent-Types.md`), e.g.:
    - `BriefWritingPattern` → always attaches citation and guardrail rule blocks.
    - `CodingAssistantPattern` → attaches Python style/guardrail blocks, different execution plan steps.
  - A `PatternSelector` that chooses the pattern from tier + agent type.

These two layers together give you:

- **Stable “prompt DSL” in files** (rule blocks and templates under `ai_components/`).
- **Programmable assembly logic** in `patterns/` that turns specs into orthogonal prompts.

If you’re happy with this direction, next concrete steps (when we’re allowed to execute again) are:

- Introduce `tests/` and a `patterns/` module.
- Create initial `ai_components/rule_blocks/*.md` by lifting content from `_DOCS` (brief agent, DSL workframe, framework graph).
- Implement `PatternSelector` + `TierXPattern` to actually use those rule blocks when rendering prompts.

### What the current implementation already supports (end‑to‑end)

- **Design flow**
  - You write a Markdown spec: `promptctl init-spec my_agent` → fill in the sections.
  - `promptctl design my_agent.agent-spec.md` then:
    - Parses the spec (`parse_agent_spec_from_markdown`).
    - Uses `PatternSelector` (backed conceptually by `patterns/tier_patterns.json`) to pick a tier/pattern.
    - `DesignAgent` builds a structured `PromptDesign` (Task, optional Plan, RuleBlock refs, tier).
    - `ProjectStorage` saves `projects/<id>/designs/design.json` and renders a minimal system prompt to `projects/<id>/prompts/system.md`.
- **Evaluation flow**
  - You provide scenarios as JSON (list of `EvaluationScenario`).
  - `promptctl evaluate <identifier> scenarios.json`:
    - Loads the saved `PromptDesign`.
    - `EvaluatorAgent` runs simple structural checks (expected properties vs task/constraints now; `evaluation/rubrics.json` defines richer rubrics for future LLM‑based evaluation).
    - Prints a report and (optionally) saves it as JSON.
  - `RefinementAgent` can turn an `EvaluationReport` into human‑readable refinement suggestions (no auto‑edit yet).

### What naturally follows next (according to the current design)

- **Wire patterns + rule blocks more deeply**
  - Implement/extend `PatternSelector` to *actually* consume `patterns/tier_patterns.json` (instead of just hard‑coded logic).
  - Create concrete Markdown rule‑block files under `ai_components/rule_blocks/` whose names match the pattern definitions (e.g. `citation_formatting_rules.md`, `brief_html_structure_rules.md`, guardrails, markup rules) by extracting them from `_DOCS` and your existing system‑prompt examples.
- **Implement a richer Evaluator using rubrics**
  - Extend `EvaluatorAgent` to
